Study location: Davies Reef lagoon (central Great Barrier Reef, Queensland, Australia); coral collection and SeaSim aquarium facilities (AIMS, Townsville, Queensland, Australia)
Sediment composition: Davies Reef sediment (primarily carbonate); clearance rate experiment C1
Sediment notes: C1 includes Porites spp treatment (mixture of P. lutea and P. lobata) due to identification issues; Table 3 lists Porites spp under C1. Numeric per species not given in provided text.

Study location: Davies Reef lagoon (central Great Barrier Reef, Queensland, Australia); coral collection and SeaSim aquarium facilities (AIMS, Townsville, Queensland, Australia)
Sediment composition: Davies Reef sediment (primarily carbonate) fine and coarse silt? S1 uses fine or coarse silt from Davies Reef with deposition 235 mg cm[−][2] day[−][1]
Sediment notes: S1: deposition of 235 mg cm[−][2] day[−][1] as fine or coarse silt left for 0,1,2,4,8,16 d followed by 42 d observation period; dark-adapted Fv/Fm measured over time for smothered and non-smothered tissue (Table 3; Fig. 7; Methods). Provided text gives ANOVA/P-values but not per-treatment means.

Study location: Magnetic Island, turbid nearshore fringing reef, Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia
Sediment composition: Fine, muddy sediment collected from 3 m depth behind the breakwater wall of the AIMS jetty; sieved through a 3-mm mesh; aerated for two days; sediment contained 2.37% carbon, 0.13% nitrogen, and 459 Ag/g phosphorous. Sediment remained on fragments during exposure.
Sediment notes: Laboratory Expt 1.1 used mean sediment load 151 ± 37 mg DW cm−2 (range 79–234 mg cm−2) (text: "It averaged 151 F 37 mg DW cm[�][2] (range: 79–234 mg cm[�][2] )."). Exposure durations: 0, 12, 18, 24, 36 h. Fv/Fm measured after sediment removal and dark adaptation; pigment and zooxanthellae measured on frozen subsamples.

Study location: Magnetic Island, turbid nearshore fringing reef, Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia
Sediment composition: Fine, muddy sediment as described for laboratory; applied as distributed evenly in water column until sediment settled after 6 h; sediment remained on fragments during exposure.
Sediment notes: Laboratory recovery assessment (Experiment 1.2): fragments exposed to 0, 12, 18, and 24 h sediment, then recovery monitored up to 7 days by repeated Fv/Fm measurements. Mean sediment load used in Expt 1.1 is stated; Expt 1.2 is described without reporting dose mean.

Study location: Magnetic Island, turbid nearshore fringing reef, Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia
Sediment composition: Fine, muddy sediment (laboratory description) applied to about 50% of colony surface (patch), leaving remaining surface clean; later repeated in field with fresh sediment exposure of 22 h.
Sediment notes: Laboratory Experiment 1.3: about 50% surface covered for 48 h; Expt 1.3 later repeated in field on four colonies each of Echinopora lamellosa and massive Porites, exposed to sediments for 22 h. For M. peltiformis, response categories are tissues: C0 (before), C end (control free of sediment), E end (edge), S end (under sediment). Table 3 provides mean differences vs Cend; no absolute DF/F mV values reported.

Study location: Great Barrier Reef (GBR), field sites on windward sides of reefs at 6–8 m depth; islands: Normandy, Green, Hay, Wilkie (inshore islands 5–8 km off coast)
Sediment composition: Freshly collected sediment from 15 m depth at study site; provided finer fraction than experimental coral depth. Sediment nutrients listed in Table 1 (C, N, P vary by island). Sediment was added to one portion of colonies as a thin layer for 22 h; controls were same colonies other parts without sediment load.
Sediment notes: Field Experiment 2.2: sedimentation applied for 22 h (treatments: control tissues before application, non-sediment areas after 22 h, and tissue underneath sediment after sediment removal). Text: "we aimed to apply amounts similar to those added in the laboratory experiments (about 200 mg cm−2)" but actual values not quantified in supplied text.

Study location: Great Barrier Reef (GBR), field sites on windward sides of reefs at 6–8 m depth; islands: Normandy, Green, Hay, Wilkie
Sediment composition: Fresh sediment collected from 15 m depth; thin layer applied to one portion of colonies for 22 h; nutrients vary by reef (Table 1).
Sediment notes: Field Experiment 2.1/2.2 use 22 h sedimentation. DF/FmV measured before, after, and in sediment-covered areas.

Study location: Great Barrier Reef (GBR), field sites on windward sides of reefs at 6–8 m depth; islands: Normandy, Green, Hay, Wilkie
Sediment composition: Fresh sediment from 15 m depth applied as thin layer to one portion of colonies for 22 h; controls are other portions of same colony.
Sediment notes: Field Experiment 2.2: DF/FmV measured in tissues not exposed vs underneath sediment after 22 h.

Study location: Great Barrier Reef (GBR), field sites on windward sides of reefs at 6–8 m depth; islands: Normandy, Green, Hay, Wilkie
Sediment composition: Fresh sediment from 15 m depth applied for 22 h to one portion of colonies; thin layer.
Sediment notes: Field Experiment 2.2; outcomes are effective quantum yield DF/FmV.

Study location: Great Barrier Reef (GBR), field sites on windward sides of reefs at 6–8 m depth; islands: Normandy, Green, Hay, Wilkie
Sediment composition: Fresh sediment from 15 m depth applied for 22 h as thin layer to one portion of colonies.
Sediment notes: Field Experiment 2.2. Three species not affected by experimental application of sediments included Fungia crassa.

Study location: Kaneohe Bay, Oahu, USA
Sediment composition: Sediments collected from the top 5 cm of sediment from the same fringing reef where coral fragments were collected; drained of excess seawater; dried for 48 h at 60°C; aliquoted into vials and sterilized via autoclaving; applied directly onto coral surface with sterile serological pipettes. (sediment was sterilized)
Sediment notes: Sediment stress infection trials: sediments were applied in 3 dosages every 3 h to reach a total dose of 1000 g m−2 d−1 for 24 h. Quote: "Sediments were applied in 3 dosages every 3 h to reach a total dose of 1000 g m[−2] ." and Fig. 2 caption "sediment loading: 1000 g m[−2] d[−1] for 24 h".

Study location: Kaneohe Bay, Oahu, USA
Sediment composition: No sediment loading (ambient conditions) compared to stressed groups; infection trial otherwise identical and included non-pathogen (FSW only) fragments in block design. (control condition)
Sediment notes: Control groups described as "non-stressed" with no sediment loading. Fig. 2 caption: "Closed shapes, solid lines: non-stressed (no sediment loading) groups".

Study location: Cockle Bay, Magnetic Island, North Queensland, Australia (19° 11.215' S, 146° 48.950' E)
Sediment composition: Fine sediment collected from surface layer near field site (Cockle Bay); particle sizes 60–120 mm (sandy silts); rinsed in freshwater, stored dry and rehydrated 1 h prior to use (low organic carbon).
Sediment notes: Laboratory experiment: high-sediment load 110 ± 27 mg cm−2; flow regime High-flow 24 ± 4 cm s−1.

Study location: Cockle Bay, Magnetic Island, North Queensland, Australia (19° 11.215' S, 146° 48.950' E)
Sediment composition: Fine sediment collected from surface layer near field site (Cockle Bay); particle sizes 60–120 mm (sandy silts); rinsed in freshwater, stored dry and rehydrated 1 h prior to use (low organic carbon).
Sediment notes: Laboratory experiment: moderate-sediment load 16 ± 4 mg cm−2; flow regime High-flow 24 ± 4 cm s−1.

Study location: north shore of Mo'orea, French Polynesia
Sediment composition: 11 g (dry mass) of sediments applied every 1–2 days; sediments from adjacent seafloor (dry mass stated; no particle size reported)
Sediment notes: Control condition described as 'no sediment addition' (Figure 1 label). Sedimentation treatment: average deposition rate 54.2 + 5.8 mg (dry mass) cm[2] d[2][1] (i.e., mg (dry mass)/cm^2/day) from 'For the ‘þsedimentation’ treatments...' in Methods.

Study location: north shore of Mo'orea, French Polynesia
Sediment composition: n/r (vermetid-only treatment: vermetids present; sedimentation not added)
Sediment notes: Treatment label corresponds to 'þvermetid' without sediment addition: four treatments defined as (i) neither stressor, (ii) sedimentation, (iii) vermetids, (iv) both stressors.

Study location: north shore of Mo'orea, French Polynesia
Sediment composition: 11 g (dry mass) sediments from adjacent seafloor applied every 1–2 days
Sediment notes: Methods: 'average sediment deposition rate of 54.2 + 5.8 (mean + 95% CI) mg (dry mass) cm[2][2] d[2][1]'. Treatment label corresponds to 'þsedimentation' without vermetids.

Study location: north shore of Mo'orea, French Polynesia
Sediment composition: 11 g (dry mass) sediments from adjacent seafloor applied every 1–2 days
Sediment notes: Both stressors: 'both stressors' treatment. Methods describe sediment deposition rate (54.2 + 5.8 mg (dry mass)/cm^2/d) and vermetids manipulation. Figure 1 notes significant interaction between vermetids and sedimentation.

Study location: Orpheus Island (18° 36′S, 146° 29′E), Australia; National Sea Simulator facility (SeaSim), Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS), Townsville, Queensland, Australia
Sediment composition: Fine particles; wet-sieved; average ± sd. particle size: 7.3 ± 1.5 μm, 95% < 20 μm; sediments collected at 2 m depth at Orpheus Island and kept in 60 l flowthrough tanks at 27 °C
Sediment notes: Suspended sediments (0, 10, 30 or 100 mg l[−][1]) fully crossed with nutrient enrichment (+0 or +0.6 mg OC l[−][1] FSW) for 40 days. Exposures: 60 μmol photons m[−][2] s[−][1] over 12 h:12 h diurnal cycle; sediments kept in suspension (VorTech MP10 pump) and aragonite substrata fixed ~3 cm below surface to prevent sediment accumulation on juveniles. Control: 0 mg l[−][1] suspended sediments and low nutrients (+0). Source: section 2.2 Experimental design and treatment types; section 2.3 Experimental setup; Abstract states exposures for 40 days.
